title: Sanford Meisner

infobox.value: February 2, 1997(1997-02-02) (aged 91)  [Sherman Oaks, Los Angeles](/wiki/Sherman_Oaks,_Los_Angeles "Sherman Oaks, Los Angeles"), California, U.S.

Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *(December 2016)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))*

(August 31, 1905 – February 2, 1997) was an American actor and acting teacher who developed an approach to acting instruction that is now known as the [Meisner technique](/wiki/Meisner_technique "Meisner technique").[[1]](#cite_note-1) While Meisner was exposed to [method acting](/wiki/Method_acting "Method acting") at the [Group Theatre](/wiki/Group_Theatre_(New_York_City) "Group Theatre (New York City)"), his approach differed markedly in that he completely abandoned the use of [affective memory](/wiki/Affective_memory "Affective memory"), a distinct characteristic of method acting. Meisner maintained an emphasis on "the reality of doing", which was the foundation of his approach.[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELongwellMeisner19879–10-2)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

American actor and acting teacher (1905–1997)

| Sanford Meisner | |
| --- | --- |
|  | |
| Born | (1905-08-31)August 31, 1905  New York City, U.S. |
| Died | February 2, 1997(1997-02-02) (aged 91)  [Sherman Oaks, Los Angeles](/wiki/Sherman_Oaks,_Los_Angeles "Sherman Oaks, Los Angeles"), California, U.S. |
| Other names | Sandy |
| Occupations | * Actor * acting teacher |
| Years active | 1924–1997 |
| Spouses | * Peggy Meredith  ​  ​ (m. 1948; div. 1950)​ * Betty Gooch (divorced) |
| Partner | James Carville |

**Sanford Meisner** (August 31, 1905 – February 2, 1997) was an American actor and acting teacher who developed an approach to acting instruction that is now known as the [Meisner technique](/wiki/Meisner_technique "Meisner technique").[[1]](#cite_note-1) While Meisner was exposed to [method acting](/wiki/Method_acting "Method acting") at the [Group Theatre](/wiki/Group_Theatre_(New_York_City) "Group Theatre (New York City)"), his approach differed markedly in that he completely abandoned the use of [affective memory](/wiki/Affective_memory "Affective memory"), a distinct characteristic of method acting. Meisner maintained an emphasis on "the reality of doing", which was the foundation of his approach.[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELongwellMeisner19879–10-2)

## Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sanford_Meisner&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life")]

Born in [Brooklyn](/wiki/Brooklyn "Brooklyn"), New York City, Meisner was the oldest child of Hermann Meisner, a [furrier](/wiki/Furrier "Furrier"), and Bertha Knoepfler, both Jewish immigrants who came to the United States from [Hungary](/wiki/Hungarian_Jews "Hungarian Jews").[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJackson2002-3) His younger siblings were Jacob, Ruth, and Robert. To improve Sanford's health during his youth, his family took a trip to the [Catskills](/wiki/Borscht_Belt "Borscht Belt"). While there, however, his brother Jacob contracted [bovine](/wiki/Bovine "Bovine") [tuberculosis](/wiki/Tuberculosis "Tuberculosis") from drinking [unpasteurized](/wiki/Unpasteurized "Unpasteurized") milk and died shortly thereafter. In an interview many years later, Meisner described this event as "the dominant emotional influence in my life from which I have never, after all these years, escaped."[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELongwellMeisner19875-4) Because he was unable to cope with feelings of guilt relating to his brother's death, for which his parents blamed him, the young Meisner became isolated and withdrawn.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

He found release in playing the family piano and eventually attended the Damrosch Institute of Music (now the [Juilliard School](/wiki/Juilliard_School "Juilliard School")), where he studied to become a concert pianist.[[5]](#cite_note-auto-5) When the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression") hit, however, his father pulled him out of music school to help with the family business in New York City's [Garment District](/wiki/Garment_District,_Manhattan "Garment District, Manhattan"). Meisner later recalled that his only means of enduring long days spent lugging bolts of fabric was to entertain himself by replaying, in his mind, all the classical piano pieces he had studied in music school. Meisner believed this experience helped him develop, at age twenty, an acute sense of sound akin to [perfect pitch](/wiki/Perfect_pitch "Perfect pitch").[[5]](#cite_note-auto-5) Later, as an acting teacher, he often evaluated his students' scene work with his eyes closed and his head dramatically buried in his hands. This trick was only partly for effect, he explained, because it actually helped him more closely listen to his student's work and pinpoint the true and false moments in their acting.[[5]](#cite_note-auto-5)

After graduating from Erasmus Hall High School in 1923, Meisner professionally pursued acting, which had interested him since his youth. He had acted at the [Lower East Side](/wiki/Lower_East_Side "Lower East Side")'s [Chrystie Street](/wiki/Chrystie_Street "Chrystie Street") [Settlement House](/wiki/Settlement_House "Settlement House") under the direction of [Lee Strasberg](/wiki/Lee_Strasberg "Lee Strasberg"), who would play an important role in his development. At age 19 Meisner heard that the [Theatre Guild](/wiki/Theatre_Guild "Theatre Guild") was hiring teenagers and, after a brief interview, was hired as an extra for *They Knew What They Wanted*. The experience deeply affected him, leading to the realization that acting had always been his life's ambition. He and Strasberg both appeared in the original Theatre Guild production of the Rodgers and Hart revue *[The Garrick Gaieties](/wiki/The_Garrick_Gaieties "The Garrick Gaieties")*, from which the song "[Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan_(song) "Manhattan (song)")" came.

## Group Theatre

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sanford_Meisner&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Group Theatre")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/09/Group-Theatre-1938.jpg/500px-Group-Theatre-1938.jpg)](/wiki/File:Group-Theatre-1938.jpg)

Sanford Meisner (back row center) with members of the Group Theatre in 1938

Despite his parents' misgivings, Meisner continued to pursue a career in acting, receiving a scholarship to study at the [Theatre Guild of Acting](/wiki/Theatre_Guild "Theatre Guild"). Here he encountered once again [Harold Clurman](/wiki/Harold_Clurman "Harold Clurman") and [Lee Strasberg](/wiki/Lee_Strasberg "Lee Strasberg"). Strasberg was to become another of the century's most influential acting theorists and the father of [method acting](/wiki/Method_acting "Method acting"), an acting technique derived, like Meisner's own, from the [system](/wiki/Stanislavski%27s_system "Stanislavski's system") of [Konstantin Stanislavski](/wiki/Konstantin_Stanislavski "Konstantin Stanislavski"). The three became friends. In 1931, Clurman, Strasberg, and Cheryl Crawford (another Theatre Guild member) selected 28 actors (one of whom was Meisner) to form the [Group Theatre](/wiki/Group_Theatre_(New_York_City) "Group Theatre (New York City)"). This company exerted an influence on the entire art of acting in the United States. Meisner summered with the Group Theatre at their 1936 rehearsal headquarters at [Pine Brook Country Club](/wiki/Pine_Brook_Country_Club "Pine Brook Country Club") in the countryside of [Nichols, Connecticut](/wiki/Nichols,_Connecticut "Nichols, Connecticut").[[6]](#cite_note-6)

Meisner, along with a number of other actors in the company, eventually resisted Strasberg's preoccupation with [affective memory](/wiki/Affective_memory "Affective memory") exercises. In 1934, fellow company member [Stella Adler](/wiki/Stella_Adler "Stella Adler") returned from private study with Stanislavski in Paris and announced that Stanislavski had come to believe that, as part of a rehearsal process, delving into one's past memories as a source of emotion was only a last resort and that the actor should seek rather to develop the character's thoughts and feelings through physical action, a concentrated use of the imagination, and a belief in the "given circumstances" of the text. As a result, Meisner began to focus on a new approach to the art of acting.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

When the Group Theatre disbanded in 1940, Meisner continued as head of the acting program at the [Neighborhood Playhouse](/wiki/Neighborhood_Playhouse "Neighborhood Playhouse") in New York, at which he had taught since 1935.[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPostlewait1998719-8) In teaching he found a level of fulfillment similar to that which he had found in playing the piano as a child. At the Playhouse he developed his own form of method acting that was based on Stanislavski's system, Meisner's training with Lee Strasberg, and on Stella Adler's revelations about the uses of the imagination. Today that approach is called the [Meisner technique](/wiki/Meisner_technique "Meisner technique"). It was during these early years at The
Neighborhood Playhouse that Meisner was briefly married to the young actress Peggy Meredith, who appeared in several Broadway productions.

The [Actors Studio](/wiki/Actors_Studio "Actors Studio") was founded in 1947 by two ex-Group Theatre actors [Elia Kazan](/wiki/Elia_Kazan "Elia Kazan") and [Robert Lewis](/wiki/Robert_Lewis_(director) "Robert Lewis (director)"), and [Cheryl Crawford](/wiki/Cheryl_Crawford "Cheryl Crawford"). Strasberg initially had not been asked to join the group, while Meisner was among the first instructors to teach at the studio. However, by 1951, after Kazan moved to [Hollywood](/wiki/Hollywood,_Los_Angeles "Hollywood, Los Angeles") to focus on his directorial career, Strasberg became the group's artistic director. In the following years, many students of the Actors Studio became well known in the film industry. Strasberg's later insistence that he had trained them distressed Meisner enormously, creating an animosity with his ex-mentor that continued until Strasberg's death.

## Neighborhood Playhouse School of the Theatre

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sanford_Meisner&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Neighborhood Playhouse School of the Theatre")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This section **needs additional citations for [verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this article](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Sanford_Meisner "Special:EditPage/Sanford Meisner") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners") in this section. Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *(December 2016)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

In 1935, Meisner joined the faculty of The Neighborhood Playhouse School of the Theatre and continued as the Director of the Acting Department until his retirement in 1990, and served as Director Emeritus until his death in 1997.[[9]](#cite_note-9) In 1928, The Neighborhood Playhouse School of the Theatre opened its doors. The first class of only nine students had the privilege of being taught by theatre luminaries [Martha Graham](/wiki/Martha_Graham "Martha Graham"), [Louis Horst](/wiki/Louis_Horst "Louis Horst"), [Laura Elliott](/wiki/Laura_Elliott "Laura Elliott"), and [Agnes de Mille](/wiki/Agnes_de_Mille "Agnes de Mille"). Over his years of teaching at the Playhouse, this founding member of The Group Theatre developed and refined what is now known as the Meisner Technique: "To live truthfully under given imaginary circumstances."[[10]](#cite_note-10) The Meisner Technique is a step-by-step procedure of self-investigation for the actor now widely recognized as one of the foremost acting techniques taught today.

Notable students and alumni of The Neighborhood Playhouse under Sanford Meisner's instruction include: [Dylan McDermott](/wiki/Dylan_McDermott "Dylan McDermott"), [James Caan](/wiki/James_Caan "James Caan"), [Steve McQueen](/wiki/Steve_McQueen "Steve McQueen"), [Robert Duvall](/wiki/Robert_Duvall "Robert Duvall"), [Gregory Peck](/wiki/Gregory_Peck "Gregory Peck"), [Diane Keaton](/wiki/Diane_Keaton "Diane Keaton"), [Jeff Goldblum](/wiki/Jeff_Goldblum "Jeff Goldblum"), [Tony Randall](/wiki/Tony_Randall "Tony Randall"), [Sydney Pollack](/wiki/Sydney_Pollack "Sydney Pollack"), [David Mamet](/wiki/David_Mamet "David Mamet"), [Connie Britton](/wiki/Connie_Britton "Connie Britton"), [Brian Geraghty](/wiki/Brian_Geraghty "Brian Geraghty"), [Leslie Moonves](/wiki/Leslie_Moonves "Leslie Moonves"), [Sherie Rene Scott](/wiki/Sherie_Rene_Scott "Sherie Rene Scott"), [Chris Noth](/wiki/Chris_Noth "Chris Noth"), [Tucker Smallwood](/wiki/Tucker_Smallwood "Tucker Smallwood"), [Mary Steenburgen](/wiki/Mary_Steenburgen "Mary Steenburgen"), [Betsy von Furstenberg](/wiki/Betsy_von_Furstenberg "Betsy von Furstenberg"), [Allison Janney](/wiki/Allison_Janney "Allison Janney"), [Jennifer Grey](/wiki/Jennifer_Grey "Jennifer Grey"), [Ashlie Atkinson](/wiki/Ashlie_Atkinson "Ashlie Atkinson"), [Christopher Meloni](/wiki/Christopher_Meloni "Christopher Meloni"), [Alex Cole Taylor](/wiki/Alex_Cole_Taylor "Alex Cole Taylor"),[[11]](#cite_note-11) and many more.

## Meisner/Carville School of Acting

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sanford_Meisner&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Meisner/Carville School of Acting")]

In 1983, Meisner and his life partner James Carville founded the Meisner/Carville School of Acting on the Caribbean island of [Bequia](/wiki/Bequia "Bequia"). Students from all around the world came every summer to participate in a summer intensive with Meisner. The Meisner/Carville School of Acting operated on the island and, beginning in 1985, also in North Hollywood. Meisner split his time between the Neighborhood Playhouse in New York and the two school locations. In spring of 1995, The Meisner/Carville School of acting was then succeeded by [The Sanford Meisner Center for the Arts](/wiki/The_Sanford_Meisner_Center_for_the_Arts "The Sanford Meisner Center for the Arts"), a theater company and school in [North Hollywood](/wiki/North_Hollywood "North Hollywood")[[12]](#cite_note-12) established by Meisner, James Carville, and Martin Barter.[[13]](#cite_note-obit-13) Graduates from Meisner's 2-year program could audition for the company. The company became a fixture on the Los Angeles theater scene for several years after Meisner's death.[[14]](#cite_note-14) Meisner attended every rehearsal and every performance until the very end.

### Notable students

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sanford_Meisner&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Notable students")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This section **needs additional citations for [verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this article](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Sanford_Meisner "Special:EditPage/Sanford Meisner") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners") in this section. Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *(August 2015)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

Throughout his career, Meisner worked with, and taught, students who became well known. [Sydney Pollack](/wiki/Sydney_Pollack "Sydney Pollack") and [Charles E. Conrad](/wiki/Charles_E._Conrad "Charles E. Conrad") served as Meisner's senior assistants. The technique is helpful not just for actors, but also for directors, writers, and teachers. A number of directors also studied with him, among them [Sidney Lumet](/wiki/Sidney_Lumet "Sidney Lumet") and [John Frankenheimer](/wiki/John_Frankenheimer "John Frankenheimer"), and writers such as [Arthur Miller](/wiki/Arthur_Miller "Arthur Miller") and [David Mamet](/wiki/David_Mamet "David Mamet"). At least 37 of the students who studied with Sanford Meisner were nominated for or won Academy Awards.[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarvilleTrost2017683–685-15)

#### List of notable students

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sanford_Meisner&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: List of notable students")]

* [Joan Allen](/wiki/Joan_Allen "Joan Allen")[[16]](#cite_note-where-16)
* [Sean Astin](/wiki/Sean_Astin "Sean Astin")
* [Alec Baldwin](/wiki/Alec_Baldwin "Alec Baldwin")
* [Roger Bart](/wiki/Roger_Bart "Roger Bart")
* [Susan Blakely](/wiki/Susan_Blakely "Susan Blakely")[[17]](#cite_note-17)
* [Jeff Bridges](/wiki/Jeff_Bridges "Jeff Bridges")
* [Connie Britton](/wiki/Connie_Britton "Connie Britton")
* [Sandra Bullock](/wiki/Sandra_Bullock "Sandra Bullock")
* [Ty Burrell](/wiki/Ty_Burrell "Ty Burrell")

## Film and television appearances

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sanford_Meisner&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Film and television appearances")]

Though he rarely appeared on film, he performed in *[Tender Is the Night](/wiki/Tender_Is_the_Night_(film) "Tender Is the Night (film)")*, *[The Story on Page One](/wiki/The_Story_on_Page_One "The Story on Page One")*, and *[Mikey and Nicky](/wiki/Mikey_and_Nicky "Mikey and Nicky")*. His last acting role was in the season one episode of the television medical drama *[ER](/wiki/ER_(TV_series) "ER (TV series)")*, titled "Sleepless in Chicago". Actor [Noah Wyle](/wiki/Noah_Wyle "Noah Wyle") worked with him and referred to the experience as the highlight of his career.

| Year | Title | Role | Notes |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 1959 | *[The Story on Page One](/wiki/The_Story_on_Page_One "The Story on Page One")* | Phil Stanley |  |
| 1962 | *[Tender Is the Night](/wiki/Tender_Is_the_Night_(film) "Tender Is the Night (film)")* | Dr. Franz Gregorovious |  |
| 1976 | *[Mikey and Nicky](/wiki/Mikey_and_Nicky "Mikey and Nicky")* | Dave Resnick |  |

## Author

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sanford_Meisner&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Author")]

Meisner is the author of the book *Sanford Meisner on Acting* (Knopf Doubleday 1987), considered by some to be an indispensable tool for contemporary actors.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

## Personal life and death

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sanford_Meisner&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Personal life and death")]

Meisner's two marriages, to Peggy Meredith (née Meyer) and Betty Gooch, respectively, ended in divorce. Meisner, who was bisexual,[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarvilleTrost201751-27) spent the remainder of his life with partner James Carville.

In 1970 Meisner was diagnosed with [throat cancer](/wiki/Throat_cancer "Throat cancer") and underwent a [laryngectomy](/wiki/Laryngectomy "Laryngectomy").[[13]](#cite_note-obit-13) After that operation he lived for nearly three more decades, until February 2, 1997, when he died in his sleep at the age of 91 at his home in Sherman Oaks, Los Angeles.[[28]](#cite_note-:0-28)

## The Meisner technique

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sanford_Meisner&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: The Meisner technique")]

Main article: [Meisner technique](/wiki/Meisner_technique "Meisner technique")

Meisner's unusual techniques were considered both unorthodox and effective. Actor Dennis Longwell wrote of sitting in on one of Meisner's classes one day, when Meisner brought two students forward for an acting exercise. They were given a single line of dialogue, told to turn away, and instructed not to do or say anything until something happened to make them say the words (one of the fundamental principles of the Meisner technique). The first student's line came when Meisner approached him from behind and gave him a strong pinch on the back, inspiring him to jump away and yelp his line in pain. The other student's line came when Meisner reached around and slipped his hand into her blouse. Her line came out as a giggle as she moved away from his touch.[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELongwellMeisner198734-29)

The goal of the Meisner technique has often been described as getting actors to "live truthfully under imaginary circumstances."[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESilverberg19949-30)
